column3: Thank you for being part of our alpha testing program!

At the Gates is still missing a couple small features (e.g. [Faction Traits|FACTION-TRAIT], game setup options) and a lot of icons, text, and interface elements but is otherwise feature-complete. Even so, the balance and pacing of the game certainly still needs work, and you're very likely to run into crashes or other bugs as you play.
If you do notice any issues, have any questions, or want to provide feedback please let us know at contact@conifergames.com so that we can help improve the game!

Because AtG is still an active development an autosave is created every turn, and at roughly 3MB each these files can add up if you play a lot. Your settings, save games, and log files can be found in your installation folder.
Thanks again for helping us out, and I hope you enjoy playing At the Gates!
- Jon
Help tips will occasionally appear here to assist you with learning game concepts.
You can open the Help Screen and see a full list of tips by clicking the [COLOR:EMPHASIS] ? [/COLOR] button in the upper-left corner of the screen, or by pressing the [HOTKEY:?] key.
Mouse over any word in [yellow|TOOLTIPS] to learn more about it - even from [inside other tooltips|TOOLTIPS]!
At the Gates is a hard, slow game. Accomplishing things will take time, and although the early game is often difficult you will eventually build a powerful kingdom.
Click the button on the next screen to continue learning more about the basics of AtG.
Want to go it alone? Feel free to skip these tips and read any of them later in the Help Screen (hotkey: [HOTKEY:?]).
At the Gates is a [turn-based|TURN] strategy game similar to other 4X games (e.g. Civilization). The 3 biggest differences are:
[*] You only ever own one 'city', your [SETTLEMENT].
[*] You'll spend more time managing [Resources|RESOURCE] and characters ([Clans|CLAN]).
[*] AtG is generally more unpredictable and 'roguelike' than other 4X games.
That last point is important - in one game you might have a huge source of [TREASURE] nearby, while in another you'll be battling the endless [winter|COLD] of the far north.
For your first couple games we've made sure the map is somewhere in-between.
Your ultimate [goal|WINNING] is to [Build a Kingdom|DECLARE-KINGDOM] powerful enough to topple the [Roman Empire|ROME], either militarily or by more indirect means.

You'll start each game by [Foraging|FORAGE] for [FOOD] before eventually [settling down|DECLARE-KINGDOM] in one place and building more advanced and permanent [Structures|STRUCTURE] from [STONE-BLOCKS].
There are also other [Factions|FACTION] in the world who have already been on the map for a while - some are immensely strong right at the start of the game.

You're not directly competing with anyone in AtG, but that doesn't mean you can ignore your neighbors. [The other Leaders|LEADER] can have strong [Personalities|LEADER-TRAIT] (and militaries) and if you upset someone you might come to regret it.
Your first order of business is deciding which of your 3 [Clans|CLAN] to [Train|TRAIN] in what [Profession|PROFESSION]. We suggest starting with an [EXPLORER].
Next, you need to choose a [Tech|TECH] to begin [Studying|STUDY]. We suggest starting with [AGRICULTURE].
These are decisions you'll be making often.
This is all you need to do on the first turn, though next turn you'll have a a shiny new [Unit|UNIT] ready to begin exploring or [Harvesting Resources|HARVEST] from the map.
The most important task you need to address will always be displayed in the glowing button in the upper-right corner of the World Screen.
Remember that you can read these tips again at any time by pressing [HOTKEY:?] from the World Screen.
Your high-level goals in the first few turns of the game are:
[*] Revealing the map around you with an [EXPLORER] in order to find [Abandoned Ruins|DESERTED-LOCATION] (which often contain useful items) and [Resource Deposits|DEPOSIT] you can [Harvest|HARVEST].
[*] [Training|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] as [Foragers|FORAGE] in order to [Harvest Deposits|HARVEST] you already know about.

For the most part you can use the map to guide your strategic decisions. Whatever [Resource Deposits|DEPOSIT] are available nearby should usually become the foundation of your economy, as even things you don't need can be traded for what you lack when a [Caravan|CARAVAN] is visiting.
At the very least you'll want a [HUNTER], [GATHERER], or [REAPER] soon, before [winter's chill|SEASON] takes hold.
There are 2 [ways to win|WINNING] in AtG:
[*] Conquering the [Capital City|CAPITAL] of one of the two [Roman Empire Factions|ROME].
[*] Becoming 'Magister Militum' and taking over [Rome|ROME] from within by sending five [Clans|CLAN] to become Roman Legions.
Note that there are two Roman [Factions|FACTION]: the Eastern and Western halves of the previously-whole-in-better-days Roman Empire, and either one can satisfy your [Victory Conditions|WINNING].

Conquering a Roman capital will require locating it on the map and significant military preparation. Although various setbacks will befall the Romans they start off quite strong and can put up quite a fight.
Becoming Magister Militum is more of an economic victory, as to send a [Clan|CLAN] to [Rome|ROME] not only means sending it off for a year but also providing it with expensive, late-game equipment.

Along the way you'll want to do your best to keep the [Leaders|LEADER] of neighboring [Factions|FACTION] happy, as they can also be very strong and dangerous if annoyed. They all vary dramatically in strength and aren't competing with you for [Victory|WINNING], but their [Personalities|LEADER-TRAIT] are all very unique and you really don't want to accidentally make the wrong enemies!
On the plus side, these [Leaders|LEADER] aren't trying to accomplish the same thing as you, but merely compete for dominance amongst themselves.
You can view and manage your pursuit of [Victory|WINNING] in the Victory Progress Screen (F7).

You'll mostly be interacting with your [Clans|CLAN], which start inside your [SETTLEMENT] and can be [Trained|TRAIN] in various [Professions|PROFESSION], some of which allow them to [Move|MOVE-POINT] around the map as [Units|UNIT].

The more [Clans|CLAN] you have the more you'll be able to do, but also the more [FOOD] your tribe will consume. New [Clans|CLAN] arrive every few turns at a rate determined by how much [FAME] you're generating.
[Resources|RESOURCE] are [Harvested|HARVEST] from map [Deposits|DEPOSIT] and [dense Forests|FOREST] by [Foragers|FORAGE], and later in the game by more efficient and expensive [Structures|STRUCTURE].

[Deposits|DEPOSIT] eventually run out, so you'll need to continuously find new ones - at least until you're able to [Construct|CONSTRUCT] highly-advanced [STONE-BLOCKS:SINGULAR]-based [Structures|STRUCTURE], which last forever.

Each Turn represents half a month, and eventually it will become [Winter|COLD]. Most sources of [FOOD] cease [Production|PRODUCE] during [Cold Months|COLD], so make sure you have enough [Turns of Food|STORED-TURN] saved up before it starts getting chilly! [Winter|COLD] is usually around 10 turns long, so try to always have at least that amount.
At the Gates has the coolest tooltips of any game ever made, so be sure to make use of them!
Move the mouse cursor over a highlighted word anywhere in the game and you can learn more - even from within other tooltips.
The game also has several hotkeys to assist in navigating the interface, which will appear when mousing over a button that has a corresponding key.
The most important keys are the spacebar, which opens the [Clans|CLAN] Screen, and ENTER, which ends the turn or activates the next unresolved high-priority notification.

The World Screen is where you'll spend most of your time, and is mostly taken up by the map. The 3 most important buttons are located in the upper-right, and involve making high-level decisions which affect your tribe. The Selection Panel appears in the lower-left corner when you click on a map object, and provides you with information and commands related to it.

The 3 most important screens in the game are the [Clans|CLAN] Screen, the [Training|TRAIN] Screen, and the [Research|STUDY] Screen. The latter two both show the ""tree"" of the [Professions|PROFESSION] that [Clans|CLAN] can be [Trained|TRAIN] in (once [Researched|LEARN]).
Most other less-important game screens can be opened via one of the small buttons at the top of the World Screen (or via their associated hotkey).

During the first few years of the game you'll be on the lookout for new places to move your [SETTLEMENT] after you've [Exhausted|EXHAUST] the [Resources|RESOURCE] in an area. This isn't necessary during the first year, but it's something to think about after your first [Winter|COLD].

After you've developed your economy a bit and found a region with several [Deposits|DEPOSIT] you'd like to [Harvest|HARVEST] forever you can use the ['Found Kingdom'|DECLARE-KINGDOM] command in the [Clans|CLAN] Screen.

This permanently glues your [SETTLEMENT] to whatever Tile it's currently on, but provides it a large increase in its [Control Level|CONTROL] ([Borders|BORDERS]) and you a large boost in [FAME] (helping you acquire new [Clans|CLAN] faster).

[Founding your Kingdom|DECLARE-KINGDOM] is a big step, and costs 20 Parchment. This is an action you probably won't be ready for until at least turn 50 - before this your goal is simply to explore the map and develop your economy in order to open up larger strategic plays in the midgame.
Each of the 10 major tribes like yourself possesses a single [SETTLEMENT], which functions similarly to cities in other 4X strategy games - the big difference is that you'll only ever have one.

The [SETTLEMENT] contains all of your tribe's [Clans|CLAN] (except for those you [Train|TRAIN] in [Active Professions|ACTIVE]). It can also Train one [Clan|CLAN] in a [Profession|PROFESSION] at a time, and because you'll only have a single [SETTLEMENT] how you choose to use this pipeline is very important.

Another unique feature of your [SETTLEMENT] compared with cities in other games is that it can [Pack Up|PACKED] and move around the map - something that will become necessary as you eventually use up the [Resources|RESOURCE] near your starting location and need access to more.
Relocating should be a careful, strategic decision though, as [Packing Up|PACKED] prevents your [SETTLEMENT] from being able to [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN].

Your [SETTLEMENT] also has a [Control Level|CONTROL], which determines how far your [Borders|BORDERS] reach, and in turn allows your [Units|UNIT] to [Construct|CONSTRUCT] [Structures|STRUCTURE] and receive free [Supply|SUPPLY] during the [Winter|COLD].

Maps in AtG are a randomly generated set of Tiles containing various types of [Terrain|TERRAIN]. Tiles can also contain [Resource Deposits|DEPOSIT] you can [Harvest|HARVEST] with [Foragers|FORAGE] and [Structures|STRUCTURE].

Each type of [Terrain|TERRAIN] has a [Movement Cost|MOVE-POINT] for [Units|UNIT] passing through, as well as a [Supply|SUPPLY] rating which determines whether or not Units can safely remain on the Tile (avoid extended journeys through [SNOW] or [DESERT]!).

A Tile's [Terrain|TERRAIN] can change as the [Seasons|SEASON] come and go, and things can get pretty bad in the [Winter|COLD] depending on how far north you are. Very few sources of [FOOD] can continue being [Harvested|HARVEST] during the [Cold Months|COLD], so be prepared!

It is also possible for bad [Weather|WEATHER] to randomly afflict Tiles, decreasing the [Supply|SUPPLY] they provide or even shutting down [Movement|MOVE-POINT] and the [Harvesting of Resources|HARVEST] (e.g. Flooding after Rain).
Your [Clans|CLAN] are the main building block of your tribe. You start with 3 and will end a (successful!) game with more than 40.

Each [Clan|CLAN] can be [Trained|TRAIN] in a [Profession|PROFESSION], which allows it to [Produce Resources|PRODUCE], do something on the map as a [Unit|UNIT], like fight and explore, or provide some other effect.

In addition to its [Profession|PROFESSION] a [Clan|CLAN] can have a [Discipline|DISCIPLINE], and can gain [Experience|XP] and [Levels|LEVEL] in it. This mostly determines how long it takes to [Train|TRAIN] the [Clan|CLAN] in a new [Profession|PROFESSION] (those in the same [Discipline|DISCIPLINE] are much faster), but a [Clan|CLAN]'s [Mood|MOOD] might be affected as well based on its [Personality Traits|CLAN-TRAIT].
Each [Clan|CLAN] has 2 [Personality Traits|CLAN-TRAIT], which can be good, bad, or simply something to plan around.
A [Clan|CLAN]'s [Mood|MOOD] is determined by 2 things: its [Traits|CLAN-TRAIT], and whether its [Desire|DESIRE] has been satisfied (when it has one).
Happy [Clans|CLAN] receive [Resource Production|PRODUCE] and combat bonuses, while unhappy Clans suffer the opposite. Most Clans will simply be content, and require no attention.

Each [Clan|CLAN] arrives with one Family and will slowly grow to include more. Families consume a small amount of [FOOD] per turn. Because their number continually increases throughout the game be mindful to always have a plan for how you'll feed hungry new mouths!
You can right-click on a [Clan|CLAN]'s 'card' to attach a color-coded note to it (e.g. to keep track of what you want to [Train|TRAIN] it as later).
Each [Clan|CLAN] can be [Trained|TRAIN] in a [Profession|PROFESSION], of which there are two basic types: [Active|ACTIVE] and [Settled|SETTLED].

Being in an [Active Profession|ACTIVE] allows a [Clan|CLAN] to perform actions on the map, such as fighting or [Harvesting Resources|HARVEST]. Most [Professions|PROFESSION] are [Settled|SETTLED], which simply means the [Clan|CLAN] remains inside the [SETTLEMENT], and either refines [Resources|RESOURCE] or provides some other kind of economic effect.
Only one [Clan|CLAN] can be [Trained|TRAIN] at a time across your entire tribe so think carefully about what you really want, and in what order.

The number of Turns it takes to [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN] is based on the Clan's [Discipline|DISCIPLINE], its [Level|LEVEL] in that Discipline, and how high the Level is of the [Profession|PROFESSION] in question. Being efficient is important, so try not to have [Clans|CLAN] switch [Disciplines|DISCIPLINE] too often, as this resets their [Level|LEVEL] back to 0.
The effectiveness of individual [Clans|CLAN] can be improved by [Researching|STUDY] and then [purchasing Profession-based Upgrades|UPGRADE].

In general, you'll want to focus on [Training|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] in [Professions|PROFESSION] which relate directly to your on-map situation (e.g. [ARCHER:S] for fighting [Bandits|BANDIT], or a [DIGGER] to [Harvest|HARVEST] a [Mineral Deposit|MINERAL]).

A [Unit|UNIT] is simply a [Clan|CLAN] which has been [Trained|TRAIN] in an [Active Profession|ACTIVE] and now capable of [Moving|MOVE-POINT] around on the map. Multiple [Units|UNIT] can be grouped together into [Stacks|STACK].

[Warrior Units|WARRIOR] can fight and capture [Civilian Units|CIVILIAN], which are defenseless and focused on economic activities like [Harvesting Resources|HARVEST]. Warriors can also capture or [Pillage|PILLAGE] enemy [Structures|STRUCTURE].

Each [Unit|UNIT] has a certain amount of [Move Points|MOVE-POINT], which determines how far it can move in a Turn. Tiles with clear, flat [Terrain|TERRAIN] cost 1 [Move Point|MOVE-POINT] to enter and most [Units|UNIT] have 3 to spend each Turn.

[Units|UNIT] also require [Supply|SUPPLY] from their Tile, and will lose [Morale|MORALE] and [Health|HEALTH] if the [Supply|SUPPLY] provided by a Tile is less than what they need (this is usually caused by changes in [Season|SEASON] or [Weather|WEATHER]).

Out on the map you'll also encounter [Units|UNIT] belonging to other [Factions|FACTION]. If you have a [Warrior Unit|WARRIOR] you can engage in combat or capture someone else's undefended [Civilians|CIVILIAN], but be mindful of who the Unit actually belongs to, as some [Leaders|LEADER] can be quite strong!
You start the game only able to [Train|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] in a few [Professions|PROFESSION] - to increase your options you need to unlock more through [Research|STUDY].

Most of the ['Techs'|TECH] you'll be [Researching|STUDY] will be [Professions|PROFESSION], but you can also unlock [Profession-based Upgrades|UPGRADE], and even a handful of global, permanent bonuses, which are usually quite expensive.
You can [Research|STUDY] one thing at a time, and at a rate determined by how much [KNOWLEDGE] you're [Producing|PRODUCE] each Turn and the cost of the project you're undertaking.

The [Tech Tree|TECH] is broken up into six branches, each based on a [Discipline|DISCIPLINE]. [Researching|STUDY] any [Tech|TECH] in a ""row"" will allow you to proceed onto ANY Tech in the next row up, so don't feel the need to Research everything - just prioritize what you need right now.

The first [Tech|TECH] in each [Discipline|DISCIPLINE] (colored branch) is special in that it doesn't unlock a new [Profession|PROFESSION] but instead allows you to choose one of your [Clans|CLAN] to [Level Up|LEVEL] in that [Discipline|DISCIPLINE] (making it faster to [Train|TRAIN] them in [Professions|PROFESSION] in the same [Discipline|DISCIPLINE]).
You can press F1 from the World Screen or use the big button in the far upper-right corner to open up the [Research|STUDY] Screen.

There are several [other tribes|FACTION] spread across the map, and you'll usually encounter a few within the first year or two of a game. Tribes are each unique in both their strength and behavior, the latter of which is determined by its [Leader|LEADER]'s [Personality|LEADER-TRAIT]. You're not competing directly with any of the other [Factions|FACTION] for [Victory|WINNING], though they can pose a serious obstacle to acquiring [Resources|RESOURCE] - and on the battlefield.

Two special [Factions|FACTION] you'll run into are the Western and the Eastern halves of the [Roman Empire|ROME]. Recent times have not been kind to the Empire, and although still strong at the start of the game they'll grow steadily weaker as the years pass. Your ultimate goal is to either (1) conquer one of their capitals, or (2) take over the Empire indirectly by sending enough [Clans|CLAN] to join it that they seize control of it.

The two other special [Factions|FACTION] are the [Bandits|BANDIT] and the [Neutrals|NEUTRAL]. Neither one has a [Leader|LEADER] or conducts diplomacy, and you're permanently at war with both. Bandits will actively come after you, while the Neutrals keep to themselves and possess various useful [Structures|STRUCTURE] around the map that you can freely ""incorporate"" into your tribe (should you defeat any defenders present).

Every [Faction|FACTION] [Leader|LEADER] has a unique [Personality|LEADER-TRAIT] which greatly shapes their behavior, particularly their Approach towards you. Keeping the other [Leaders|LEADER] happy (especially those much stronger than you!) is an important element of the game. Giving everyone what they want can be difficult though, as Leaders will often want contradictory things.

Your [Relationship Level (RL)|RL] with another [Leader|LEADER] measures how much they like you, and ranges from -10 to +10. [Influence|INFLUENCE] is usually earned alongside [RL|RL], but is instead ""spent"" like money to get a Leader to do something for you. [Reputation|REPUTATION] is global, hard to earn, and can be spent as Influence with ANY Leader. [Leverage|LEVERAGE] is the total of all 3 of these stats and is what is checked when determining if a Leader will ultimately agree to one of your proposals.

When you initially make contact with a [Leader|LEADER] a 'First Meeting' event occurs. The Leader chooses how they will greet you (with a gift, a demand, or simply saying hello) based on their [Personality|LEADER-TRAIT], and you have a choice of how to respond. This gives you an early opportunity to shape a relationship in your favor with a gift of your own - or perhaps earning some Reputation points with a well-timed insult!

One of your most important jobs is preventing your tribe from [Starving|STARVE]. You start with some [FOOD] in your [Stockpile|STOCKPILE], but not enough to survive the [Winter|COLD], so make sure you start collecting more within a few months with a [Forager|FORAGE] like a [GATHERER] or [HUNTER].

Other important early-game [Resources|RESOURCE] include [KNOWLEDGE], which is what allows you to [Research|STUDY] new [Professions|PROFESSION], and [FAME], which helps you acquire new [Clans|CLAN] faster. You can also use [TREASURE] to buy [Resources|RESOURCE] or sell ones you have too much of when the [Caravan|CARAVAN] is visiting. [CLOTH] is necessary to expand your tribe's [Popcap|POPCAP] so that it can continue growing and adding new [Clans|CLAN].
You have one [SETTLEMENT] which can [Train|TRAIN] one [Clan|CLAN] in a [Profession|PROFESSION] at a time, so try to plan ahead as much as possible.

[Training|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] as [Foragers|FORAGE] is important early on, as you want to start [Harvesting Resources|HARVEST] from the map as soon as possible. Later in the game you'll be able to [Produce Resources|PRODUCE] much more efficiently by having [Clans|CLAN] [Construct|CONSTRUCT] [Structures|STRUCTURE] instead.

[Researching|STUDY] new [Professions|PROFESSION] and their [Upgrades|UPGRADE] is important to keeping your economy and military on-track. Your best bet is to prioritize getting a basic [Resource|RESOURCE]-refinement pipeline set up (e.g. [Harvesting|HARVEST] [IRON] and then turning it into [WEAPONS] with your initial [Research|STUDY] choices, as this will give you something to sell to the [Caravan|CARAVAN] in exchange for whatever else you might lack.

[Resources|RESOURCE] are one of the 2 fundamental building blocks of the game's economy ([Clans|CLAN] being the other one). Your main source will be [Harvesting Deposits|HARVEST], though it is possible to Harvest [TIMBER] from [dense Forests|FOREST].

You'll use [Foragers|FORAGE] to [Harvest your Resources|HARVEST] the first couple years, before you start [Constructing|CONSTRUCT] [Structures|STRUCTURE], and can supplement this [Production|PRODUCE] with purchases from the [Caravan|CARAVAN] if it's visiting.

[Foragers|FORAGE] and [TIMBER]-based [Structures|STRUCTURE] [Deplete|DEPLETE], and then eventually [Exhaust|EXHAUST] the available [Resources|RESOURCE] on a Tile, so you'll usually need to move your [SETTLEMENT] a couple times in order to find more.
In the mid-game you'll be able to [Found a Kingdom|DECLARE-KINGDOM], settle down in one place, and [Construct|CONSTRUCT] [STONE-BLOCKS:SINGULAR]-based [Structures|STRUCTURE] that last forever.
[FOOD] is the most important [Resource|RESOURCE], as if you ever run out your tribe will [Starve|STARVE].

[TREASURE] is also very useful, as it can be used to buy a variety of [Resources|RESOURCE] from the [Caravan|CARAVAN] (as well as upgrade it with new wares), and even ply neighboring [Leaders|LEADER] with gifts.
Your [FAME] is what determines how often new [Clans|CLAN] join your tribe, and there are a handful of [Professions|PROFESSION] which can help you [Produce|PRODUCE] more.
There are 3 types of [Resources|RESOURCE]: [Plants|PLANT], [Animals|ANIMAL], and [Minerals|MINERAL]. Each is useful in different ways.

[Harvesting Resources|HARVEST] is important, but you'll eventually want to also Refine them into more advanced [Resources|RESOURCE] (such as [IRON] into [WEAPONS], or [WOOL] into [CLOTH]). Many [Professions|PROFESSION] you can unlock though [Research|STUDY] relate to [Resource|RESOURCE] refinement, so refer to the tree for ideas on what strategies you can pursue.

[FOOD] is an essential [Resource|RESOURCE] which your [Clans|CLAN] eat each turn. Should you run out your tribe will [Starve|STARVE], reducing [Resource Production|PRODUCE], [Morale|MORALE], and more. You can [Harvest|HARVEST] it from a variety of map-based [Deposits|DEPOSIT] with the help of a [Forager|FORAGE]. The [Caravan|CARAVAN] occasionally has [FOOD] for sale, but don't expect to be able to reliably feed your [Clans|CLAN] this way.

[FOOD] is different from other [Resources|RESOURCE] in that it enters your [Stockpile|STOCKPILE] as [""Stored Turns""|STORED-TURN] rather than a fixed, spendable quantity. You consume exactly 1 [Turn of Food|STORED-TURN] each Turn. The amount of raw [FOOD] above your needs you're able to [Produce|PRODUCE] is turned into surplus turns you can spend later (100% excess [Production|PRODUCE] equals +1 [Stored Turn|STORED-TURN]).

In the first few turns it's important to pick a [Deposit|DEPOSIT] (usually a [Plant|PLANT]) you'll use to feed your growing tribe, and [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN] as a [Forager|FORAGE] to [Harvest|HARVEST] it. This should last you a year or two before running out, when you'll need to find a new, larger source (and maybe something you can refine into even more).

You can [Produce Resources|PRODUCE] from map-based [Deposits|DEPOSIT] and [dense Forests|FOREST] with a [Clan|CLAN] [Trained|TRAIN] in a [Foraging|FORAGE] [Profession|PROFESSION]. Once your economy is more advanced you can [Produce|PRODUCE] even more by having a Clan in an appropriate [Profession|PROFESSION] (e.g. [FARMER], [MINER]) [Construct|CONSTURCT] a [Structure|STRUCTURE] there.

Many [Deposits|DEPOSIT] start off as [Unidentified|UNIDENTIFIED], and you'll need a [Forager|FORAGE] of the right type (e.g. a [DIGGER] for a [Mineral Deposit|MINERAL]) or a more advanced [SURVEYOR] figure out what they are before you can [Harvest any Resources|HARVEST] from them.

[Harvesting Resources|HARVEST] from a Tile [Depletes|DEPLETE] and eventually completely [Exhausts|EXHAUST] it. The only way to avoid this is by [Constructing|CONSTRUCT] advanced [STONE-BLOCKS:SINGULAR]-based [Structures|STRUCTURE], which both last forever and can have their [Production|PRODUCE] increased by assigning [Apprentices|APPRENTICE] to them.

Another way to increase [Resource Production|PRODUCE] is by [Training|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] in 'synergy' [Professions|PROFESSION] which boost the output of other Clans (e.g. Bread Maker). You can also improve individual Clans by [Researching]|STUDY] and then [purchasing Profession-based Upgrades|UPGRADE] (using a button that appears on the [Clan|CLAN] Card).
[Warrior Units|WARRIOR] have a few important stats.

First is [Power|POWER], which is a general metric of how strong a [Unit|UNIT] is in combat. In general, one Unit or [Stack of Units|STACK] with double the [Power|POWER] of another will beat up their foe but not quite kill them. Defenders can receive bonuses for the [Terrain|TERRAIN] they're on, or for having Dug In over multiple turns.

[Health|HEALTH] is another important stat. It starts at 100 and if it reaches 0 a [Unit|UNIT] dies. As it drops it also lowers a Unit's [Power|POWER]. Avoiding [Health|HEALTH] loss is especially important on long campaigns, as [Units|UNIT] don't automatically recover Health, and can only do so in the [SETTLEMENT] by spending [OIL] and [CLOTH].

[Morale|MORALE] is similar to a [Health|HEALTH], except that it recovers automatically each turn. Attacking [Units|UNIT] lose more Health in combat, while defenders lose more [Morale|MORALE]. The goal of the attacker is to reduce the defender's Morale to 0, which causes them to chaotically retreat from their Tile, lose any defensive bonuses they had, and lose a huge amount of [Health|HEALTH] as they flee.

Another important factor to consider in warfare is [Supply|SUPPLY]. Each Tile provides a certain amount automatically which drops during the [Winter|COLD], and when without Supply for more than one turn a [Unit|UNIT] will lose [Morale|MORALE] and [Health|HEALTH]. [Encamping|ENCAMP] temporarily provides a Unit with [Supply|SUPPLY], but makes it extremely vulnerable to attack, so plan your campaigns carefully!

You'll spend most of your time in the World Screen looking at and interacting with the Map (with most of the rest being spent managing [Clans|CLAN]). Clicking on a Tile with a non-[Terrain|TERRAIN] object will bring up the Selection Panel, where you can learn more about the object in question and possibly interact with it (e.g. telling a [Unit|UNIT] to start [Harvesting|HARVEST]).

The 3 most important buttons are in the upper-right corner. The largest one is the ""Task Button"", which automatically points you toward the next important decision for the turn, or ends the turn if everything is addressed. This is also where important notifications will appear. The other two big buttons allow you to easily choose a [Clan|CLAN] to [Train|TRAIN] in a [Profession|PROFESSION] and choose a new [Tech|TECH] to [Research|STUDY].
There are various small buttons on the World Screen which will open screens which provide more info or provide other useful tools, such as a notepad to keep track of your thoughts.
There are also several other cool interface features you can learn more about by selecting ""Advanced Interface Concepts"" below.

The most important screen in the game besides the World Screen is the [Clans|CLAN] Screen. You can press the spacebar to open or close it. This screen shows you all of the [Clans|CLAN] in your [SETTLEMENT], which you can [Train|TRAIN] in various [Professions|PROFESSION]. It also includes a few important but rare tribe-level actions, such as [Founding a Kingdom|DECLARE-KINGDOM]. You'll spend a lot of time in this screen!

The two other important screens are the [Training|TRAIN] Screen (accessed from the [Clans|CLAN] Screen) and the [Research|STUDY] Screen (hotkey: F1). Both show you the [Professions|PROFESSION] you can choose to [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN] in or Research. The Research Screen also includes a number of other bonuses you can acquire besides unlocking new [Professions|PROFESSION].

The [Victory|WINNING] Screen (F6) shows you how to win the game and how close you are to doing so. The Diplomacy Screen (F3) allows you to speak with other [Leaders|LEADER]. The [Clan|CLAN] Info Screen (F8) gives you a single place where you can keep track of your [Clans|CLAN], both inside and outside the [SETTLEMENT].

AtG's tooltips are the game's most powerful tool for learning how to play. Simply move your mouse over any EMPHASISed word in the game and it will create a new tooltip explaining the concept - even from within other tooltips!

A few of the especially-fancy tooltips like those for [Professions|PROFESSION] can be customized - right from within the tooltip itself. Sections can be collapsed or even (almost) hidden completely, if say, you're already familiar with the basic descriptions for everything and just want to see the cold, hard numbers.
You can reset your tooltip settings in the Options Screen (Ctrl-O), or even customize them individually by modifying the Settings.xml file in your AtG installation folder.
Any hotkey which opens a screen may also be used to close it (e.g. pressing spacebar will both open and close the [Clans|CLAN] Screen).
You can turn on or off the map grid or change the thickness of the gridlines by pressing 'G'.

There are several places in the game where you can write small notes to yourself to keep track of things. Right-clicking on either a [Clan|CLAN] Card or a map Tile (when nothing is selected) will allow you to attach a note to that [Clan|CLAN] or Tile. The Clan Card notes are especially useful, as this adds a colored label right to the card itself. You can use the button in the upper left corner of the World Screen or press F5 to write game-wide notes for yourself.

Major [Factions|FACTION] can become the owner of a Tile by having their [SETTLEMENT] or other [Structures|STRUCTURE] nearby. A Tile must already be owned and inside your [Borders|BORDERS] in order to [Construct|CONSTRUCT] a [Structure|STRUCTURE] there. Tiles within your Borders also always provide full [Supply|SUPPLY] regardless of the [Season|SEASON] or [Weather|WEATHER], making them an important strategic asset, especially on defense.

The [Control Level|CONTROL] of a [Structure|STRUCTURE] or [SETTLEMENT] is equal to the [Border|BORDERS] distance, and this can be upgraded by spending [WEAPONS] to [Fortify|FORTIFY] it. The cost of doing so increases each time though, so only do so strategically.

Note that [Foragers|FORAGE] may [Harvest Resources|HARVEST] from any Tile on the map, regardless of whether or not it's inside your [Borders|BORDERS] - don't forget that they still need [Supply|SUPPLY] though!
[Structures|STRUCTURE] may be built by [Clans|CLAN] [Trained|TRAIN] in special [Professions|PROFESSION] that have the ability to do so (e.g. [FARMER], [MINER]).
Enemy [Structures|STRUCTURE] can be [Pillaged|PILLAGE] by [Warrior Units|WARRIOR], and even seized permanently, although the output of [Occupied|OCCUPIED] Structures is greatly diminished.

[Constructing|CONSTRUCT] a [Structure|STRUCTURE] costs either [TIMBER] or [STONE-BLOCKS], depending on whether you want a basic (temporary) Structure or an advanced (permanent) one. [STONE-BLOCKS:SINGULAR]-based Structures are incredibly useful, as they last forever and can even have Apprentices assigned to them to increase their [Production|PRODUCE], but they're quite expensive so don't expect to have any until at least turn 50.

[TIMBER] [Structures|STRUCTURE] are also useful, but it usually makes sense to [Train|TRAIN] one or two [Foragers|FORAGE] first, as this allows your economy to get off the ground without committing you too far in any direction. Investing heavily in [Structures|STRUCTURE] can make sense when you start in or near a large [Forest|FOREST].

A Tile contains one or more types of [Terrain|TERRAIN], and this combination determines how difficult it is to [Move|MOVE-POINT] into, how much [Supply|SUPPLY] it provides [Units|UNIT], and which types of [Resources|RESOURCE] and [Deposits|DEPOSIT] can appear there.

[Rivers|RIVER] are an important map barrier which you'll want to plan around carefully. [Units|UNIT] require a full extra turn to enter a Tile with a Small River, and Large Rivers are impossible to cross - except in the [Winter|COLD], when [Rivers|RIVER] can freeze and essentially become flat [Terrain|TERRAIN] with no additional [Move cost|MOVE-POINT].

There are a variety of other [Seasonal|SEASON] and unpredictable [Weather|WEATHER] effects which can affect a Tile, including Blizzards, Floods, and Droughts. AtG's map is very dynamic, and you need account for this in your plans. A particularly harsh [Winter|COLD] can unravel even well-prepared rulers both economically and on the battlefield.

The changing of the [Seasons|SEASON] affects a Tile in 3 ways: whether or not [FOOD] can be [Harvested|HARVEST], the amount of [Supply|SUPPLY] available to [Units|UNIT], and how difficult it is to [Move|MOVE-POINT] into. [Weather|WEATHER], such as Rain and Blizzards can also randomly strike Tiles and dramatically affect their [Terrain|TERRAIN].

In general, when a Tile becomes [Cold|COLD] it stops [Producing|PRODUCE] [FOOD] (Berries and Meat are exceptions). When it gets even colder all available [Supply|SUPPLY] disappears (except inside your [Borders|BORDERS]). When a Tile turns into [SNOW] it becomes harder to [Move|MOVE-POINT] through, but once [Rivers|RIVER] have frozen they become much easier to cross.
Here is a full list of [Seasonal|SEASON] and [Weather|WEATHER] effects:
[*] [Cold|COLD]
[*] [BLIZZARD]
[*] [RAIN]
[*] [FLOODED]
[*] [SCORCHED]

The [Caravan|CARAVAN] is very important, as it allows you to exchange [Resources|RESOURCE] you have too much of for those you need. This means you can build up your economy to [Harvest|HARVEST] [WOOL] from [SHEEP], turn that into [CLOTH], and then trade it away for [WEAPONS] if you so desire. Don't be afraid to go all-in on a [Resource|RESOURCE]!

The only downsides are that the [Caravan|CARAVAN] isn't always available, and its stock of [Resources|RESOURCE] starts off small. You can only exchange Resources with the Caravan 3 times per year (spring, summer, and autumn), so make those visits count.

The initial stock of [Resources|RESOURCE] available at the [Caravan|CARAVAN] is quite limited, but by spending [TREASURE] to upgrade it you can dramatically increase both the quantity and quality of what it sells. If you need more of something or something that's not yet available spending [TREASURE] to upgrade the [Caravan|CARAVAN] can be a wise investment.

If a [Unit|UNIT] ends its Turn on a Tile which provides less [Supply|SUPPLY] than it requires (usually 1) then it has a one-turn 'grace period' before it starts losing [Morale|MORALE] and [Health|HEALTH].

When it's not [Winter|COLD] the only Tiles which you need to pay attention to in this regard are [DESERT:S]. This changes completely once it starts getting [Cold|COLD] though - wintertime means thinking about the map in a completely new way.

Tiles within your [Borders|BORDERS] always provide as much [Supply|SUPPLY] as needed. To avoid taking damage when Supply is lacking a [Unit|UNIT] may [Encamp|ENCAMP]. This also provides the Unit with enough [Supply|SUPPLY] for an additional turn, allowing it to continue with whatever task it was occupied with. This slows [Units|UNIT] down a bit, but the real risk is that it makes them extremely vulnerable to attack, so avoid [Encamping|ENCAMP] when enemies might be nearby.

It's possible to eliminate the [Supply|SUPPLY] available on a Tile and thus grind a powerful enemy army down by Besieging them. This requires having at least 1 more [Warrior Unit|WARRIOR] than your foe, but can be an excellent way of taking down [Bandit Camps|BANDIT] and other strongholds and taking almost no damage in the process.

One last thing to keep in mind is that although advanced [Cavalry Units|CAVALRY] are both fast and strong they also require 2 [Supply|SUPPLY] instead of 1, so keep this in mind when building and commanding your armies.

You've now seen all of the game's help tips, so from here your primary tool to continue learning about the game is the tooltip system. If you're ever confused or have further questions feel free to ask our community over on the official At the Gates forums.
We hope you enjoy the game, and good luck in your quest to topple Rome!
Move the mouse cursor over any word [in yellow|TOOLTIPS] to learn more about it.
Your first order of business is [Training|TRAIN] one of your 3 [Clans|CLAN] so they can start [Harvesting|HARVEST] [FOOD] for your growing tribe.
Press [HOTKEY:Esc] to close this message box, then press [HOTKEY:Spacebar] to open the Clans Screen.

Click on [Clan|CLAN] Relindis to the right, and then choose the [HUNTER] [Profession|PROFESSION] to start [Training|TRAIN] Relindis in it. Unlike other [Clans|CLAN] they start in the [HONOR] [Discipline|DISCIPLINE] so this will take much less time than ususal.
Close the Clans Screen, then look at the map around your [SETTLEMENT].
You have [HORSES] next to your [SETTLEMENT] which Relindis can start [Foraging|FORAGE] [MEAT] from next turn.
Go back into the Clans Screen by pressing [HOTKEY:Spacebar].
Click on the 'Leave' button underneath Clan Wilmot. They arrived already in the [WOOD-COLLECTOR] [Profession|PROFESSION].

Once they've left your [SETTLEMENT] and are able to move around the map right-click on the [Forest|FOREST] tile to the southwest of your [SETTLEMENT] to move them there. We'll start collecting [TIMBER] next turn.
The only other thing you need to do this turn is decide which [Tech|TECH] to start [Studying|STUDY].
Close this message box, then press the button that says 'Not Studying Profession' in the upper-right corner of the screen.

Click on [DISCOVERY] to start [Studying|STUDY] it. This will take one turn, and once finished you'll be able to start Studying new [Professions|PROFESSION] within the [DISCOVERY] [Discipline|DISCIPLINE].
Starting with Discovery is often smart, as this also will allow you to [Train|TRAIN] an [EXPLORER] more quickly and get a head start on identifying nearby opportunities and threats.
After you close the Research Screen press the button which says 'Finish Turn'.
Welcome to At the Gates!
AtG is a survival strategy game in which you play as a barbarian tribe trying to...
[*] Stay alive in the early game by keeping your [Clans|CLAN] from [Starving|STARVE] while [Migrating|PACKED] in pursuit of [Resources|RESOURCE].
[*] [Settle Down|DECLARE-KINGDOM] and build a proper kingdom in the midgame.
[*] Train a strong army and eventually defeat the decadent [Romans|ROME] in the endgame.
To learn more about any [EMPHASISed concept|TILE] simply hover your mouse over it. You can then hover over other EMPHASISed words, and go as deep (or not) as you want!
You can also revisit any of these tutorial messages, including optional ones you chose not to view by clicking on the '?' button in the upper left.
The core of the game is your [SETTLEMENT] and the [Clans|CLAN] it contains. Click on the large 'house' button in the upper-right to see your [Clans|CLAN].
Taking up most of the screen is the map, which is made up of [Tiles|TILE] and where most of the fun stuff happens.
The most important thing on the map is your [SETTLEMENT], as it contains all of your [Clans|CLAN].
The map also contains other [Structures|STRUCTURE], [Resource Deposits|DEPOSIT], friendly and enemy [Armies|ARMY], and more (e.g. [Deserted Locations|DESERTED-LOCATION]).
Any time you click on something on the map its details and any associated actions will appear in the lower-left corner of the screen.
Mousing over a [Tile|TILE] on the map will cause its details to appear both over the [Tile|TILE] itself and in the lower-right corner of the screen.
The most important buttons in the interface are in the upper-right corner of the screen.
The button which looks like a big house takes you to the 'Clan Management Screen'. This is the hub of your tribe, and the most important screen in the game.
Other buttons in this area allow you to expand your tribe's [Clan Limit (popcap)|POPCAP], and modify which [Profession|PROFESSION] you're currently [Studying|STUDY] and [Training|TRAIN] a Clan in.
The large rectangular button in the upper-right is the 'Turn Agenda Button', which will always direct you towards what's important to pay attention to right now.
Notifications slide in on the right side of the screen, and inform you of important events. Some Notifications are 'blocking', which means they must be addressed before you can end the [Turn|TURN].
In the upper-left corner of the screen is a list of your [Resources|RESOURCE], which may be spent on various economic activities.

There are also a few other miscellaneous buttons here, which will take you to the system menu, bring up the game's 'notepad', and more. The '?' button will take you to the Game Help Screen, where you can read any Game Tip again if you'd like.

Most things do have explanatory tooltips so if you're ever confused look for text with a red drop-shadow, as mousing over it will provide a basic explanation. Note that this works even [COLOR:EMPHASIS]inside[/COLOR] other tooltips!
Popups and screens (including this one!) can be closed by pressing [HOTKEY:Esc]. If no screen is active [HOTKEY:Esc] will open the System Menu where you can save or load a game, quit, and so on.
You can move the camera using [HOTKEY:W][HOTKEY:A][HOTKEY:S][HOTKEY:D], the arrow keys, or by putting your cursor near the edge of the screen (though this method is a bit tricky in windowed mode).
Pressing [HOTKEY:G] will toggle the visibility of the grid, and pressing [HOTKEY:1] will cycle its opacity, making it lighter or darker.
There are two [Turns|TURN] per month, and 24 each year. Which season it is greatly affects how the game plays ([Farms|FARM] stop producing [FOOD] during the winter, for example).

Your top priority during your first year is finding a reliable source of [FOOD] you can [Forage|FORAGE] for before winter arrives. Acquiring other [Resources|RESOURCE] such as [TIMBER] can also be quite useful, as this will allow you to start building [Structures|STRUCTURE].

You'll also want to start exploring your immediate vicinity in order to find any [Deserted Locations|DESERTED-LOCATION] and [Resource Deposits|DEPOSIT] that might be nearby (as well as threats, such as [BANDIT-CAMP:S]).
Your Tribe has been collecting [Rumors|RUMOR] so you start with a few 'hints' as to what's out there in the world.
This is your [SETTLEMENT], the economic and social hub of your Tribe - as nomads at heart you'll only ever have one, so make sure to take good care of it!
Living inside are your [Clans|CLAN], who can help explore, develop, and conquer the world after each is [Trained|TRAIN] in a [Profession|PROFESSION].
You can click the big 'house' button in the upper-right or press [HOTKEY:Spacebar] to view new Clans and manage the ones you already have.

One of the most significant differences between AtG and other strategy games is that [SETTLEMENT:S] are capable of [Packing Up|PACKED] and [Moving|MOVE-POINT] between [Tiles|TILE] - however, a Packed Settlement can do nothing BUT move, so make sure to plan your moves carefully, as wasting turns will set back your economic development.
This screen shows your [Settled|SETTLED] [Clans|CLAN], which are those in your [SETTLEMENT] able to be [Trained|TRAIN] in a new [Profession|PROFESSION].
Your Settlement can only [Train|TRAIN] one [Clan|CLAN] at a time, and you almost always want to be doing so!
[Clans|CLAN] appear automatically at a rate determined by how much [FAME] you're generating.
At first Clans will appear fairly regularly, but over time you'll need to increase your Fame by an ever-greater amount in order for new recruits to hear of your deeds and seek out your leadership.
Each [Clan|CLAN] is unique. Some start in a [Discipline|DISCIPLINE] like [AGRICULTURE] or [METALWORKING], which makes it easier to [Train|TRAIN] them in [Professions|PROFESSION] of the same type.
[Clans|CLAN] also have their own personalities, and 2 Traits which differentiate them from other Clans.

These Traits can be good things, like starting with free [Levels|LEVEL] in a [Discipline|DISCIPLINE], but can also be bad, like taking longer to [Train|TRAIN] in new [Professions|PROFESSION]. There are several dozen Traits in the game, and almost every [Clan|CLAN] is special in some way or another.
[Clans|CLAN] contain 1 [Family|FAMILY] when they arrive, and over time will eventually grow to contain 10.
Each [Family|FAMILY] costs 1 [FOOD] per [Turn|TURN]. Thus while older [Clans|CLAN] can be quite valuable for all of the [XP|XP] they've accumulated over time this does come at a price.
[Professions|PROFESSION] allow Clans to do several things.

Clans in [Active|ACTIVE] Professions can roam the map to fight or explore or build Structures, while those which remain in Settled Professions can [Produce|PRODUCE] or refine [Resources|RESOURCE] or provide other kinds of economic benefits.
Once trained in a [Profession|PROFESSION] a [Clan|CLAN] gains [XP|XP] in its [Discipline|DISCIPLINE] each [Turn|TURN].
Clans can switch Disciplines, but doing so resets their [Level|LEVEL] to 0. This is an especially serious penalty for very experienced Clans!
The more advanced the [Profession|PROFESSION] the higher the [Level|LEVEL] it requires.

You can still [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN] in a Profession of a higher Level, but doing so will require more [Turns|TURN] - sometimes significantly more. It is a good idea to plan out ahead of time which Professions you want which Clans to serve in.
Because one of your early priorities is exploring your immediate surroundings training a [Clan|CLAN] as an [EXPLORER] might be wise.
Ideally you want to pick a [Clan|CLAN] already in the [DISCOVERY] [Discipline|DISCIPLINE], as this will reduce the number of [Turns|TURN] needed to [Train|TRAIN] it!

The large button in the upper-right which currently says 'Choose Profession' is the 'Turn Agenda' button. It either tells you what needs immediate attention this turn or allows you to proceed to the next turn if all pressing matters have been addressed.
When in doubt, let this button be your guide for what you should do next!
This is the '[Study|STUDY] Screen'. It looks very similar to the '[Training|TRAIN] Screen', but instead shows the [Professions|PROFESSION] you can unlock over the course of the game.
Your tribe can [Study|STUDY] one new [Profession|PROFESSION] at a time.

The number on the right side of each button shows how many [Turns|TURN] of [Study|STUDY] are needed to [Learn|LEARN] that [Profession|PROFESSION], and make it possible to [Train|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] in it. The amount of time it takes is based on how much [KNOWLEDGE] it requires and how much you're producing each [Turn|TURN].
Click on the button in the lower-left to toggle how many [Professions|PROFESSION] are shown. By default, you only see those which you can [Study|STUDY] right now.
Once there are more [Professions|PROFESSION] available than can fit on the screen you can scroll through them with the mouse wheel, the arrow keys, or [HOTKEY:W]/[HOTKEY:S].

You start off producing only 1 [KNOWLEDGE] per [Turn|TURN], but by [Training|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] in certain [Professions|PROFESSION] (like the [LOREKEEPER]) you can dramatically increase this and [Learn|LEARN] things more quickly.
Note that to [Learn|LEARN] a [Profession|PROFESSION] you must [Study|STUDY] it for a minimum of 4 [Turns|TURN], regardless of how much [KNOWLEDGE] you're [Producing|PRODUCE].
Once you've [Learned|LEARN] a new [Profession|PROFESSION] you can start [Training|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] in it. Make sure you can afford the [Resources|RESOURCE] it costs to do so though!

Once you've [Learned|LEARN] a [Profession|PROFESSION] in one 'tier' of the research tree you can then [Study|STUDY] any [Profession|PROFESSION] in the next tier, allowing you to skip the others in the same tier if you'd like and move on to more advanced [Professions|PROFESSION].
Some [Professions|PROFESSION] also allow you to [Study|STUDY] others related to it.
This screen shows the [Professions|PROFESSION] you can [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN] in. Your Tribe can Train one Clan at a time.

It looks very similar to the '[Study|STUDY] Screen', and like it, the number on the right side of each button shows how many turns it takes to [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN] in that [Profession|PROFESSION] (if you opened this screen by clicking on a specific Clan).
There are two types of [Professions|PROFESSION] you can [Train|TRAIN] [Clans|CLAN] in: [Active|ACTIVE] and [Settled|SETTLED].
Being in an Active Profession allows a Clan to move around the map, either to explore, fight, or [Harvest|HARVEST] [Resources|RESOURCE] from [Deposits|DEPOSIT] on the map.

Clans in Settled Professions remain in the [SETTLEMENT], and typically provide economic bonuses, such as converting [IRON] into [WEAPONS] or increasing the rate at which you [Learn|LEARN] new Professions.

This screen is also where you can tell a [Settled|SETTLED] [Clan|CLAN] to stop working (usually only when in a [Profession|PROFESSION] that [Consumes|CONSUME] [Resources|RESOURCE] you want to preserve), or an [Active|ACTIVE] Clan which has temporarily rejoined the [SETTLEMENT] to return to the map.
Click on the button in the lower-left to toggle how many [Professions|PROFESSION] are shown. By default, you only see those which you can [Train|TRAIN] a [Clan|CLAN] in right now.
Once there are more [Professions|PROFESSION] available than can fit on the screen you can scroll through them with the mouse wheel, the arrow keys, or [HOTKEY:W]/[HOTKEY:S].
Your Tribe only has a certain number of [Stored Turns|STORED-TURN] of [FOOD], and once you run out your people will [Starve|STARVE].
Making sure this doesn't happen is very important!
During the first year or two most of your [FOOD] will come from [Foraging|FORAGE], with the help of basic [Professions|PROFESSION] like the [GATHERER], [HARVESTER], and [HUNTER].

Once you start [Harvesting|HARVEST] [TIMBER] you'll be able to [Construct|CONSTRUCT] [Structures|STRUCTURE], which are superior to the economic output of [Foragers|FORAGE] because they both [Produce|PRODUCE] more [Resources|RESOURCE] and also provide [Control|CONTROL] (which can be further expanded by spending [WEAPONS].
However, [Structures|STRUCTURE] can only be [Constructed|CONSTRUCT] on [Tiles|TILE] which you already [Control|CONTROL], so make sure to plan the placement of your [SETTLEMENT] accordingly.

After a few years you'll be able to start creating [STONE-BLOCKS] (or purchase them from [Caravans|CARAVAN]), which will allow you to [Construct|CONSTRUCT] permanent [Structures|STRUCTURE], which are extremely valuable because they not only [Produce|PRODUCE] a great deal of [Resources|RESOURCE], but they also never [Degrade|DEGRADE].
There are several [Unidentified Deposits|UNIDENTIFIED] near your starting location. They appear dark and 'shrouded' when first discovered.
You can narrow down what an [Unidentified Deposit|UNIDENTIFIED] is based on its appearance ([Plant|PLANT], [Mineral|MINERAL], or [Animal|ANIMAL]), but it can't be used until Identified.
The only way to Identify an [Unidentified Deposit|UNIDENTIFIED] is by having a [Forager|FORAGE], [Builder|CONSTRUCT], or [SURVEYOR] inspect it.
You should generally start doing so as soon as possible, as all successful empires are built on a steady flow of [Resources|RESOURCE]!

[Deposits|DEPOSIT] can provide you with [Resources|RESOURCE] useful in [Training|TRAIN], [Construction|CONSTRUCT], and trade. You can [Harvest|HARVEST] them by [Foraging|FORAGE] or by Constructing [Structures|STRUCTURE] on them.

[Plants|PLANT] are Harvested by [Farms|FARM], [Animal Herds|ANIMAL] by [Pastures|PASTURE], and [Minerals|MINERAL] by [Mines|MINE]. [TIMBER] is [Harvested|HARVEST] from [Forests|FOREST] both on and [COLOR:EMPHASIS]next to[/COLOR] [LOGGING-CAMP-1:S], rather than from specific [Deposits|DEPOSIT].

[Structures|STRUCTURE] can only be [Constructed|CONSTRUCT] by [Clans|CLAN] in the appropriate [Profession|PROFESSION]: Farms by the [FARMER], Pastures by the [RANCHER], Mines by the [MINER], and so on.

A [Clan|CLAN] which has finished [Constructing|CONSTRUCT] a [Structure|STRUCTURE] then leaves the map and becomes its resident. The [Clan|CLAN] can return to the map at any time, but the [Structure|STRUCTURE] will be destroyed in the process.

[Deposits|DEPOSIT] are eventually [Depleted|DEPLETE] after a couple of years as the supply of [Resources|RESOURCE] on its [Tile|TILE] is used up. You will eventually need to migrate in search of new [Deposits|DEPOSIT] to [Harvest|HARVEST] - keep your eye out for new [Resources|RESOURCE] you might be able to build your economy around!
You've found a [Deserted Location|DESERTED-LOCATION]!
Deserted Locations are scattered around the map, and visiting one with an [EXPLORER] can provide [Resources|RESOURCE], useful information, and sometimes even free [Clans|CLAN].
There are five types of [Deserted Locations|DESERTED-LOCATION], each offering a different set of possible rewards.
Some (like 'Deserted Farms') are common and provide useful but small rewards like a small stockpile of [FOOD].
Others (like 'Deserted Cities') are quite rare and valuable, so when you see one make sure to grab it before someone else does!
Most [Deserted Locations|DESERTED-LOCATION] are safe, but don't let your guard down as some types can contain hidden danger.
Don't ignore any [Deserted Location|DESERTED-LOCATION] completely though - with great risk comes the possibility of great reward!

Because of your primitive level of technology any time you [Harvest|HARVEST] a [Resource|RESOURCE] it will slowly [Degrade|DEGRADE] and eventually run out completely. Once the [Resources|RESOURCE] in an area (especially sources of [FOOD]) are sufficiently exhausted you'll need to migrate somewhere else.
To move you'll need to first [Pack Up|PACKED] your [SETTLEMENT], which takes a [Turn|TURN]. While Packed your Settlement won't be able to [Train|TRAIN] any [Clans|CLAN], so plan your moves carefully.

[Structures|STRUCTURE] [Constructed|CONSTRUCT] from [STONE-BLOCKS] do not [Deplete|DEPLETE] [Resources|RESOURCE], making them extremely valuable and essential to establishing a [permanent kingdom|DECLARE-KINGDOM]. Eventually, you'll want all of your [Resources|RESOURCE] to be [Harvested|HARVEST] by Stone [Structures|STRUCTURE], but you're still 50-100 [Turns|TURN] away from being able to do this right now.
You can learn more about anything on the map by mousing over its [Tile|TILE].
If a [Tile|TILE] contains several items clicking again will cycle through them.

You can also find at-a-glance info about a [Tile|TILE]'s [Terrain|TERRAIN], available [Supply|SUPPLY], [Move Cost|MOVE-POINT], etc. in the quick reference panel in the bottom-right corner of the screen.
[Armies|ARMY] outside of your [Control|CONTROL] no longer automatically have [Supply|SUPPLY]. The EMPHASISed [Tile|TILE] is an example.
The hover help box in the bottom-right corner of the screen will show you the [Supply|SUPPLY] available on a [Tile|TILE].
Soldiers will freely collect the [Supply|SUPPLY] they need when inside your [Control|CONTROL] or on favorable [Terrain|TERRAIN] (such as [FERTILE] [Tiles|TILE] in the summer).

However, [Forests|FOREST], [HILL:S], [MARSH:S] and other rough [Terrain|TERRAIN] often do not provide this benefit. Certain [Professions|PROFESSION] require different amounts of [Supply|SUPPLY], so be sure to pay attention!
An [Army|ARMY] can be without [Supply|SUPPLY] for 1 [Turn|TURN] without consequences, but it will suffer [Damage|DAMAGE] and lose [Morale|MORALE] at the end of every subsequent [Turn|TURN].
This is normally not an issue during the warm months of th eyear, but during winter make sure to keep a close eye on your Supply.
'Orange Crates' means a [Tile|TILE] will not provide the selected [Army|ARMY] with sufficient [Supply|SUPPLY]. 'Red Crates' indicates that the grace period has passed.
There is no penalty for passing [COLOR:EMPHASIS]through[/COLOR] such [Tiles|TILE], but try to avoid ending your [Turn|TURN] on any of them!
Your ??? is on a [Tile|TILE] that doesn't meet its [Supply|SUPPLY] needs.
It can remain here for one [Turn|TURN] without consequences, but any more and it will suffer [Damage|DAMAGE] and lose [Morale|MORALE].

One way to protect an [Army|ARMY] is by [Encamping|ENCAMP] it. Doing so consumes a full [Turn|TURN] and greatly reduces its [Defense Power|DEFENSE], but it's a vital tool when exploring or campaigning during the winter!
You've found a group of Bandits!
You can always recognize Bandits by their black flag.
Bandits aren't very smart, but they're reckless brutes who love killing and plundering. Make sure you protect your defenseless [Structures|STRUCTURE] and [Civilians|CIVILIAN] from them!
You've found a [Structure|STRUCTURE] owned by a [Neutral Tribe|NEUTRAL]!
Neutral [Structures|STRUCTURES] like this are scattered around the map, and can be recognized by their white flag.

You can capture [Neutral|NEUTRAL] [Structures|STRUCTURES] without a fight or any negative consequences, so don't be afraid to fuel your early growth at their expense (unless you simply feel bad for them!).
After you capture a [Structure|STRUCTURE] you are given the choice of keeping it or [Pillaging|PILLAGE] it.
[Pillaging|PILLAGE] a [Structure|STRUCTURE] provides an immediate lump sum of [Resources|RESOURCE].
Holding onto a [Structure|STRUCTURE] allows you to enjoy all the benefits of owning it after a few [Turns|TURN] of it being [Offline|OFFLINE] due to Occupation.
It's usually better to own a [Structure|STRUCTURE], but [Pillaging|PILLAGE] it can be a good option if you're too far from home or in desperate need of Resources.
Winter is coming.
[Foragers|FORAGE] and [Farms|FARM] stop producing [FOOD] once their [Tile|TILE] becomes [Cold|COLD]. Your [Clans|CLAN] will starve if you run out of Food during the frigid months to come.
Winter also eliminates the free [Supply|SUPPLY] [Armies|ARMY] rely on when moving through [Terrain|TERRAIN] you don't [Control|CONTROL] (such as on [FERTILE] [Tiles|TILE]).
Trudging through deep snow is not easy, and [Armies|ARMY] which try will quickly bog down.
On the bright side, the [Rivers|RIVER] and [MARSH:S] that normally act as significant barriers become easy to cross once frozen, so don't be afraid to take advantage of new opportunities.
That said, winters can be harsh - don't be caught unprepared!
Your supply of [FOOD] is dwindling!
Each of your [Clans'|CLAN] [Families|FAMILY] [Consumes|CONSUME] 1 [FOOD] per [Turn|TURN].
If you can't afford this you will stop accumulating [FAME] and [KNOWLEDGE], and eventually [Clans|CLAN] will suffer [Damage|DAMAGE] and be generally less effective.
Congratulations, you have identified an [Unidentified Deposit|UNIDENTIFIED] as ???!
You can now have a [Clan|CLAN] [Trained|TRAIN] in the appropriate [Profession|PROFESSION] start [Harvesting|HARVEST] it by [Foraging|FORAGE] or [Constructing|CONSTRUCT] a [Structure|STRUCTURE] on it.
[Foraging|FORAGE] can be performed on any [Tile|TILE], anywhere, but this doesn't provide very many [Resources|RESOURCE].

On the other hand, [Constructing|CONSTRUCT] a [Structure|STRUCTURE] is a much better long-term investment, but requires more advanced [Professions|PROFESSION], spending [Resources|RESOURCE], and the [Deposit|DEPOSIT] in question must be within your [Control|CONTROL] (which often requires moving your [SETTLEMENT]).
[Structures|STRUCTURE] [Constructed|CONSTRUCT] from [TIMBER] slowly [Degrade|DEGRADE] over time as they use up the [Resources|RESOURCE] on their [Tile|TILE].
Above each [Structure|STRUCTURE] is a bar showing how long it will be until this occurs. The EMPHASISed ??? will suffer this fate in ??? [Turns|TURN].

A big part of AtG is continually finding and [Harvesting|HARVEST] new [Deposits|DEPOSIT] - this isn't a game where you can just sit back, relax and expect a happy ending. Always be on the lookout for new opportunities!

You've found [the Romans|ROME]! Well, some of them, anyways. There are actually two distinct and independently-run [Roman Empires|ROME]. Both are quite strong in the early going, and picking a fight with them is very dangerous.
Your ultimate goal is to grow strong enough to take on one of the [Roman Empires|ROME] directly and capture their Capital [CITY].
In time you'll grow much stronger and the [Romans|ROME] much weaker, but for now it's definitely best to stay on their good side.
You've found ???, another major Barbarian Tribe!
They are one of the several opponents you're competing with over land, resources and glory.
You can (and will) battle against other kingdoms, but diplomatic cooperation can also be fruitful - especially when times are tough.

Because AtG is still in development, full diplomacy has not yet been implemented. You can't make agreements with other kingdoms, and they won't yet declare war on you, though other Leaders will say hello from time to time.
That said, this doesn't mean YOU can't declare war though! Feel free to do so in the Diplomacy Screen ([HOTKEY:Ctrl-D]) if you're itching for a fight.
An enemy [Army|ARMY] is within attack range!

When on offense, your goal is to reduce the defender's [Morale|MORALE] to 0. This causes them to [Retreat|RETREAT] to an adjacent [Tile|TILE], and inflicts a significant amount of [Damage|DAMAGE] on them.
Battles between two [Armies|ARMY] with equal [Power|POWER] are usually won by the defender, so always try to outnumber your enemy.

Attacking [Armies|ARMY] take much more [Damage|DAMAGE] than defenders, which unlike [Morale|MORALE] does not automatically regenerate. Before striking, make sure you're strong enough to punch through and drive the enemy back!
Also be mindful of positioning.
Being on a [Forest|FOREST] or [HILL] helps the defender, while being on a [River|RIVER] or [MARSH] hurts them.
The only exception is [Tiles|TILE] containing [CITY:S] or [SETTLEMENT:S], which always provide a large [Defense|DEFENSE] bonus.
Your ??? has entered a [Tile|TILE] [Controlled|CONTROL] by another Leader. This is not a friendly gesture, and he will not forget it.
Any text which has a red drop shadow may be moused over to reveal additional information.
Any user interface element that is gold-colored is clickable. Sometimes mousing over something in the UI will cause buttons to apepar.

Some of the more advanced tooltips in the game (such as those for [Professions|PROFESSSION] can be customized. Clicking on the thin gold bars allows you to expand hidden sections, and the buttons in the upper-right corner of a section allow you to minimize or hide it.
Clan, tile, and game notes.
Press [HOTKEY:Ctrl] to see the date and turn number.

When you capture a [CITY] or [SETTLEMENT] it becomes 'Occupied', which basically means it's of no economic value to you. However, controlling such locations will at least prevent them from training new [Armies|ARMY] to fight you!
Be wary though, as an Occupied [CITY] or [SETTLEMENT] will revert to its original owner at the end of your [Turn|TURN] if left unguarded.
Holding significant chunks of enemy territory for more than a few [Turns|TURN] is a major investment, and usually not worth the cost!
It's probably about time to start thinking about settling down and [Declaring a Kingdom|DECLARE-KINGDOM].

column4: Agreeable Leaders are likely to see the good sides of people and opportunities, rather than the risks or dangers."
Ambitious Leaders aim for the stars, and are inclined to take chances in order to achieve great success."
Ascetic Leaders care nothing for the material goods of this world, and have no need of gifts."
Belligerent Leaders are more likely to make [Demands|DEMAND], and less likely to give gifts and generally be nice to deal with."
Benevolent Leaders generally go easy on defeated foes, and are more likely to shrug off betrayal."
Calm Leaders can be firm, but generally oppose violence and argument."
Cautious Leaders don't make rash decisions, and generally try not to rock the boat when it comes to diplomacy."
Competitive Leaders want to be the best, and will see another strong Leader as a natural rival.

column3: TEXT.Description.LeaderTrait.Confident","

column4: Confident Leaders are generally optimistic and go into everything with a sense that things will work out, be it a war or an offer of friendship."
Cruel Leaders relish opportunities to be vicious and abrasive."
Cynical Leaders don't believe in much in this world, and aren't inclined to offer anyone the benefit of the doubt.

column3: TEXT.Description.LeaderTrait.Daring","

column4: Daring Leaders are likely to explore, take a risk, and start a fight, though they don't generally care much about what the end goal is."
Decadent Leaders are greedy, especially for gifts and tribute."
Deceitful Leaders can't be trusted. They'll sometimes honor alliances - and sometimes not."
Erratic Leaders are generally unpredictable. They might give you valuable gifts, or they might try to kill you. Watch out!"
Faithless Leaders place almost zero importance on [Religion|RELIGION], and don't care about what anyone else believes."
Greedy Leaders lust after material wealth, making them easily bribable.

column3: TEXT.Description.LeaderTrait.Haughty","

column4: Haughty Leaders believe they're the best, and everyone else is inferior."
Helpful Leaders seek out opportunities to do good in a cruel, mean world."
Honest Leaders are reliable and can be trusted to follow through on their word."
Jealous Leaders become upset when you act friendly with their enemies.

column3: TEXT.Description.LeaderTrait.Logical","

column4: Logical Leaders are consistent and predictable in their behavior, nearly always playing the odds."
Meek Leaders are inclined to think things won't work out, and back down instead."
Nervous Leaders rarely make bold moves, and are usually easily bullied."
Pious Leaders care a great deal about [Religion|RELIGION], both internally and what other Leaders believe."
Pragmatic Leaders take a matter-of-fact approach to life, pursuing opportunities and avoiding dangers based on the situation."
Protective Leaders are concerned about losing what they have, and have a rather guarded approach to the world.

column3: TEXT.Description.LeaderTrait.Reserved","

column4: Reserved Leaders don't like to talk, and will seldom contact you."
Sensitive Leaders take everything very seriously and personally, good and bad."
Sociable Leaders like to talk, and will contact you frequently."
Stoic Leaders take life as it comes, and don't get too impressed or upset by much of anything."
Stubborn Leaders stick with a decision no matter what, regardless of consequences."
Suspicious Leaders are unlikely to agree to much, and have a hard time fully trusting anyone or anything."
Vindictive Leaders never forgive, and never forget. So much as annoying them and you've made an enemy for life."

column5: Just so you know, I no longer particularly care about your situation.
Having thought about it I no longer feel your situation is a priority. Farewell for now.
Be aware that I'll have my eye on you going forward.
I recommend you listen to my advice from now on. It's -  for your own good.
I have some things I need to attend do, but I trust I can rely on you behaving yourself for a little while -  right?
It's time for me to take care of some business, so be a good boy and don't misbehave while I'm occupied.
I don't like you. So watch yourself.
You've caught my eye, and not in a good way. I advise you to change your ways.
You are a vile person who will burn in the fires of hell one day. Get out of my sight.
I can no longer ignore your crimes. I vow to crush you some day, one way or another.
Should there be anything you need my assistance with please don't hesitate to ask!
Oh, hey, I'm here if you need me. Just so you know.
We have some business to take care of together, do we not? I'm looking forward to our partnership!
I have a good feeling about the two of us working together.
The world out there is a cruel one, so know that I'll lend a hand if I can.
If you ever need assistance don't be afraid to ask. I'm happy to help.
My friend! I just want you to know how appreciative I am of our bond, and hope it grows even stronger in the future.
I want to let you know that I truly appreciate your support, and I promise to prioritize our relationship.
Let's settle this on the battlefield.
The time for talk is over.
If you know what's good for you you'll offer me a token of your appreciation.
If you were to offer a nice gift it would help make sure we won't have any trouble. Understand?
Greetings, scum. You know your place, right? I'm generously offering you the chance to show you know your place.
Show your master how appreciative you are that he allows you to live.
Good. I think we'll get along rather well.
A wise choice. I'll let you know what I have other matters for you to attend to.
Is that so? You might regret that.
Hmmm, I'm not sure I heard you. Maybe I'll give you another chance later.
You fool. You have no idea how big of a mistake you've made.
How dare you. You'll regret this, I promise you.
Greetings, stranger. I seek a fair and friendly relationship with you. Are you of similar mind?
A pleasure to meet you. I hope that our interactions will be mutually beneficial.
I'll send word should anything important come up.
Good. Let me know if you ever have a matter you'd like to discuss.
Ah, unexpected but appreciated. Thank you.
Thank you, I welcome it.
Greetings, great leader. I have heard of your great deeds and come with a gift.
Hello friend, it's nice to meet the one so many rumors have been spreading about. I have something for you.
Hmmm.
Very well then.
Thank you for your generosity.
I accept it with gratitude.
How dare you. You'll regret this.
You dare response to my gesture this way?
Greetings my friend! I just wanted to stop by and offer you a small token of my appreciation for the strong bond we share.
A pleasure to see you again. I've brought you a gift as a token of our friendship which I hope you'll accept.
Until we speak again.
Farewell for now.
Hmmm, very sorry to hear that.
I see. How disappointing.
Such an insult. So be it then.
You dare response to my gesture this way?
I think it's time you offered me a token of your appreciation for the fact that I'm so nice to you.
There's something I'd like to decorate my great hall with - you'll cover this expense for me, right?
Perfect.
Good boy.
You may regret that.
Very well, if you're willing to pay the price later.
How dare you.
What? You...
I believe it's time for us to walk together in the spiritual journey laid before us. Do you agree?
The Lord wishes to help guide you. Will you heed his call?
It pleases me to hear you'll now be walking the righteous path.
Bless you.
I see. May your soul one day be guided to the light.
I shall pray for you then.
Then you shall burn.
Damnation awaits your eternal soul.
You are a villain and should be recognized as such!
Your crimes and bad behavior must be called out, and I am doing so now!
You are a strong, wise leader and should be regarded as such!
Your great deeds should be known across the world!
You have entered my sovereign territory without my permission. I will not forget this.
Stopped by for a visit, it seems? You did not have permission to do that.
You're getting a little too close to my territory.
You need to keep your distance.
Perfect.
Good boy.
You may regret that.
Very well, if you're willing to pay the price later.
How dare you.
What? You...
Back again, it seems. I see now that your words mean nothing.
You can't stay away, can you? It seems you are not true to your word.
I need some bandits cleared out. Please take care of it before my next visit a year from now.
There are vermin which need killing. I'll verify you've completed this task durin my next visit a year from now.
I'll be back in a year.
See you next year then.
I see. How disappointing.
What a shame, I expected better of you.
You will regret saying that.
You will wish you hadn't said that.
Were you able to eliminate the bandits?
Did you destroy the bandit camp as I requested?
Perfect.
Good boy.
What? Are you really so useless?
Worthless.
You dare speak to me in such a way?!
You fool. You will pay for this.
My ministers are saying we have a bit of a tax shortfall this year. This is something you can help me fix, correct? Let's say, 100 Treasure. I'll give you a year to prepare things.
There's something expensive I'd like to decorate my great hall with - you'll cover this expense for me, right? It will cost 100 Treasure. There's no hurry though. A year should be enough time, right?
I'll be back in a year.
See you next year then.
I see. How disappointing.
What a shame, I expected better of you.
You will regret saying that.
You will wish you hadn't said that.
So, do you have something for me?
I'm looking forward to receiving your gift.
Perfect.
Good boy.
What? Are you really so useless?
Worthless.
You dare speak to me in such a way?!
You fool. You will pay for this.
It seems some bandits have intercepted our food shipments out to the frontier. You can help make up this shortfall though, right? 20 Food, I think it was. I'll give you a year to prepare things.
I'd like to hold a feast - I can trust you'll be able to assist me with this, right? I think I'll need 20 Food. There's no hurry though. A year should be enough time, right?
I'll be back in a year.
See you next year then.
I see. How disappointing.
What a shame, I expected better of you.
You will regret saying that.
You will wish you hadn't said that.
So then, is the food I requested last year ready to be picked up?
Is the food I asked for ready?
Perfect.
Good boy.
What? Are you really so useless?
Worthless.
You dare speak to me in such a way?!
You fool. You will pay for this.
